title: Wine Bottle Guide
subtitle: Shapes, sizes, and why they matter

overview.title: The Evolution of the Wine Bottle
overview.description: The wine bottle is perhaps the most underappreciated piece of wine technology. For millennia, wine was stored in clay amphorae, animal skins, and wooden barrels. The glass bottle as we know it emerged only in the 17th century, and standardization took another two hundred years. Today's bottle shapes carry centuries of regional tradition, practical innovation, and marketing evolution.

key_milestones.characteristics: Pointed bottoms for sand storage, sealed with clay or resin
key_milestones.limitation: Fragile, heavy, porous without proper sealing

key_milestones.characteristics: Barrels for bulk transport and storage, skins for personal use
key_milestones.limitation: Wine oxidized quickly once barrel tapped, no long-term storage

key_milestones.characteristics: Onion-shaped, squat bodies, uneven thickness, used for serving not storage
key_milestones.limitation: Expensive, irregular shapes couldn't lie flat, weak at neck

key_milestones.characteristics: Taller, straighter sides emerged, could be binned on their side
key_milestones.limitation: Still hand-blown, significant variation between bottles

key_milestones.characteristics: Consistent 750ml capacity, regional shapes codified, mass production
key_milestones.advancement: Cork closures perfected, cellar aging becomes practical

key_milestones.characteristics: Lightweight bottles, screw caps, environmental considerations
key_milestones.advancement: Closure diversity, sustainability focus, alternative formats

why_750ml.title: The 750ml Mystery

theories.name: Lung Capacity Theory
theories.explanation: A glassblower could produce roughly 750ml of bottle volume with one breath
theories.plausibility: Possible but unproven; hand-blown bottles varied significantly

theories.name: British Imperial Gallon
theories.explanation: 6 bottles (6 x 750ml = 4.5L) fit neatly into an Imperial gallon case structure

theories.name: French-British Trade
theories.explanation: A 225L Bordeaux barrel divides evenly into 300 bottles of 750ml

theories.name: EU Harmonization
theories.explanation: 750ml became mandatory for EU wine trade in 1970s

why_750ml.conclusion: The 750ml size likely emerged from a combination of practical glassblowing limits, trade mathematics, and was formally codified in the 20th century.

shapes.title: Regional Bottle Shapes
shapes.description: Wine bottle shapes evolved regionally, each designed for practical reasons that became tradition. The shape often hints at what's inside.

bottles.name: Bordeaux
bottles.practical_reason: High shoulders catch sediment when pouring aged wines

bottles.name: Burgundy
bottles.shape: Sloping shoulders, wider body, gentle curves
bottles.color: Dark green (red), pale green or clear (white)
bottles.practical_reason: Pinot Noir produces less sediment; sloped shoulders aid stacking

bottles.name: Alsace / Mosel
bottles.practical_reason: Designed for river transport; tall shape fit more bottles in boats

bottles.name: Champagne
bottles.shape: Heavy, thick glass, deep punt, sloping shoulders
bottles.practical_reason: Thick glass withstands 6 atmospheres of pressure; deep punt adds strength

bottles.name: Rhone
bottles.shape: Similar to Burgundy but with embossed coat of arms
bottles.practical_reason: Embossed papal keys or coat of arms indicate regional authenticity

bottles.name: Port / Sherry
bottles.shape: Bulbous neck (Port) or straight (Sherry), pronounced shoulders
bottles.practical_reason: Dark glass protects from light; bulbous neck catches heavy sediment

bottles.name: Provence Rose
bottles.practical_reason: Clear glass displays the pale salmon color; distinctive shape aids branding

bottles.name: Bocksbeutel
bottles.practical_reason: Protected designation; distinctive shape prevents counterfeiting
bottles.history_note: The name may derive from 'Bocksbeutel' (goat scrotum) referring to the shape, though some scholars dispute this etymology

bottles.name: Fiasco
bottles.shape: Round-bottomed bulb wrapped in straw basket (fiasco)
bottles.practical_reason: Straw basket protects fragile round bottom, allows bottle to stand upright
bottles.history_note: The iconic straw-wrapped fiasco was the symbol of Italian wine for decades. Modern Chianti has largely abandoned it in favor of Bordeaux-style bottles to signal quality
bottles.decline_reason: Labor-intensive straw weaving, association with cheap wine, impossibility of cellar storage on side

bottles.name: Clavelin
bottles.practical_reason: 620ml capacity reflects the amount remaining after 6+ years of evaporative aging (part des anges)
bottles.history_note: The only legally mandated non-standard wine bottle size in France. The 620ml represents what remains of one liter after years of oxidative aging under voile yeast
special_features: Protected shape for Vin Jaune only

bottles.name: Antinori / Super Tuscan
bottles.shape: Heavy, elongated Bordeaux-style with exaggerated shoulders and thick base
bottles.practical_reason: Heavy weight signals premium quality; deep punt adds perceived value
bottles.history_note: Emerged with the Super Tuscan movement as producers sought to differentiate from traditional Chianti. The heavy, dark bottle became a marker of serious, age-worthy wine

bottles.name: Tokaji / Cotnari
bottles.shape: Tall, slender, similar to Alsace but often 500ml
bottles.practical_reason: 500ml format reflects the precious nature of botrytized wine and allows multiple tastings from harvest
bottles.history_note: The 500ml size for Tokaji dates to the Austro-Hungarian Empire. Modern producers sometimes use 375ml or 750ml, but 500ml remains traditional

bottles.name: Ice Wine / Dessert
bottles.practical_reason: Half-size reflects labor-intensive production and precious nature of dessert wines
bottles.history_note: The 375ml format became standard for German Prädikatswein as production yields decreased with sweetness level

bottles.name: Bag-in-Box
bottles.practical_reason: Bladder collapses as wine is dispensed, preventing oxidation for weeks. Lower carbon footprint than glass
bottles.history_note: Invented by Thomas Angove in Australia in 1965. Long associated with cheap wine but increasingly used for premium wines, especially in Scandinavia
special_features: Wine stays fresh 4-6 weeks after opening
80% lower carbon footprint than equivalent glass

bottles.name: Cidre / Pet-Nat
bottles.shape: Similar to Champagne but often lighter weight
bottles.practical_reason: Crown caps allow easy disgorgement and are cheaper than corks for low-pressure sparkling
bottles.history_note: Crown caps have been revived by the natural wine movement for pétillant naturel. They signal informal, minimal-intervention winemaking

sizes.title: Bottle Sizes
sizes.description: Larger formats age more gracefully. Smaller bottles age faster. The relationship between wine volume and oxygen exposure through the cork creates different aging trajectories.
sizes.intro_note: Named after biblical kings and figures, large format bottles are prized for aging and celebration.

naming_history.title: Biblical Names for Big Bottles
naming_history.explanation: The tradition of naming large bottles after biblical figures began in 19th century Champagne houses. The grandeur of biblical kings seemed appropriate for bottles of increasing magnificence. The names progress roughly chronologically through the Old Testament.
naming_history.cultural_note: Bordeaux and Champagne use different names for some sizes, reflecting their separate traditions. A Jeroboam in Champagne (3L) is different from a Jeroboam in Bordeaux (4.5L).

figures.name: Jeroboam
figures.biblical_reference: First King of the Northern Kingdom of Israel after Solomon's death

figures.name: Rehoboam
figures.biblical_reference: Son of Solomon, King of Judah who saw the kingdom split

figures.name: Methuselah
figures.biblical_reference: Grandfather of Noah, oldest person in the Bible (lived 969 years)

figures.name: Salmanazar
figures.reign: Several kings bore this name; Shalmaneser V (727-722 BCE) most relevant

figures.name: Balthazar
figures.biblical_reference: Traditional name of one of the Three Wise Men (Magi)
figures.significance: Bringer of myrrh to the infant Jesus

figures.name: Nebuchadnezzar
figures.biblical_reference: King of Babylon who destroyed Jerusalem's Temple

figures.name: Melchior
figures.biblical_reference: Traditional name of another of the Three Wise Men
figures.significance: Bringer of gold to the infant Jesus

sizes.name: Magnum
sizes.aging_impact: Ideal for aging; slower, more graceful development

sizes.name: Jeroboam
sizes.note: Same size as Double Magnum but term used for Champagne

sizes.name: Rehoboam
sizes.named_after: King of Judah, son of Solomon

sizes.name: Imperial
sizes.named_after: Oldest person in the Bible (969 years)

sizes.name: Balthazar
sizes.named_after: One of the Three Wise Men

sizes.name: Melchior
sizes.named_after: One of the Three Wise Men

sizes.name: Melchizedek
sizes.common_use: The largest Champagne bottle produced commercially
sizes.named_after: Biblical king-priest of Salem, 'King of Righteousness'
sizes.special_note: Drappier Champagne pioneered this size; each bottle weighs over 50kg full

champagne_vs_bordeaux.title: Champagne vs Bordeaux Naming
champagne_vs_bordeaux.description: The same names don't always mean the same sizes between Champagne and still wines from Bordeaux.

differences.name: Rehoboam
differences.bordeaux: Not commonly used for this size

champagne_vs_bordeaux.why_different: Champagne houses developed their own traditions independently from Bordeaux. The added complexity of producing sparkling wine in large formats may have influenced sizing decisions.

aging_explanation.title: Why Size Affects Aging
points: All bottles have roughly the same size cork
Larger bottles have less oxygen exposure per volume of wine
Slower oxidation means more gradual, complex development
Magnums are considered ideal: practical size with aging benefits
Very large formats are often filled from standard bottles, affecting consistency

manufacturing.title: How Bottles Are Made
manufacturing.description: Modern wine bottles are made through a precise industrial process that has evolved from centuries of glassblowing tradition. Understanding glass manufacturing explains why bottles look and behave as they do.

glass_composition.title: What Glass Is Made Of

components.purpose: Flux - lowers melting temperature from 1700°C to 1500°C
Stabilizer - prevents glass from dissolving in water
Create green, amber, or dead-leaf colors

glass_composition.recycled_content: Modern wine bottles typically contain 30-90% recycled glass (cullet), reducing energy consumption by 2-3% per 10% cullet added

colors.common_use: Premium wines, particularly Champagne and Burgundy

colors.additive: No colorants, often decolorized with selenium/cobalt

manufacturing_process.title: The Bottle-Making Process

steps.name: Batch House
steps.description: Raw materials are weighed, mixed, and conveyed to the furnace. Recycled cullet is added here.

steps.name: Furnace
steps.description: Materials melt at 1500°C for 24-48 hours. Glass exits as a continuous stream at ~1100°C.

steps.name: Gob Forming
steps.description: Molten glass is cut into 'gobs' (specific weight portions) by shears.

steps.name: IS Machine
steps.description: Individual Section (IS) machine shapes bottles through blow-and-blow or press-and-blow process.

steps.name: Annealing
steps.description: Bottles pass through lehr (annealing oven), slowly cooling to relieve internal stresses. Takes 30-60 minutes.

steps.name: Inspection
steps.description: Automated cameras check for defects: cracks, bubbles, dimensional accuracy, finish quality.

steps.name: Packaging
steps.description: Bottles are palletized, often shrink-wrapped, and shipped to wineries.

manufacturing_process.speed: Modern IS machines produce 300-500 bottles per minute per section, with machines having 8-16 sections

terms.definition: The lip/mouth of the bottle where the closure seals. Critical for cork or screw cap sealing.
The top of the finish that contacts the closure. Must be perfectly flat.
The internal diameter of the neck opening. Standard is 18.5mm for cork.
The indentation in the bottom. Adds strength and aids sediment collection.
How deep the punt goes. Champagne bottles have very deep pushups.
Varies from 2mm (lightweight) to 5mm+ (Champagne). Affects strength and weight.
Tiny bubbles in glass - not a defect unless affecting structure.
Unmelted material inclusions - can cause breakage, a serious defect.

science.title: Why Bottles Matter
science.description: Beyond tradition, bottle design serves practical purposes that affect how wine ages and tastes.

factors.explanation: Dark glass blocks UV rays that can cause 'lightstrike' - a fault that gives wine a wet cardboard or cabbage smell. White wines in clear glass are more vulnerable.
factors.detail: Brown glass blocks 90% of UV; green blocks 50%; clear blocks almost none

factors.explanation: High Bordeaux shoulders catch sediment when pouring. Sloped Burgundy shoulders don't need this since Pinot Noir produces less sediment.
factors.detail: Aged Bordeaux benefits most from high-shoulder design

factors.explanation: The indent in the bottom strengthens the bottle, especially important for sparkling wines under pressure. Also aids pouring grip and sediment collection.
factors.detail: Champagne bottles have deep punts; still wine punts are often shallow or absent

factors.explanation: Champagne bottles weigh 900g vs 400g for still wine. The thick glass contains 6 atmospheres of pressure - three times a car tire.
factors.detail: Lighter bottles are becoming common for environmental reasons in still wines

factors.explanation: Standard heights allow efficient case packing and cellar storage. The Alsace flute was designed for river transport efficiency.
factors.detail: Most wine racks are designed for Bordeaux-height bottles

lightstrike_note.title: Lightstrike
lightstrike_note.description: UV light triggers a chemical reaction with riboflavin and amino acids in wine, creating sulfur compounds. The result smells of wet cardboard, cooked cabbage, or burnt rubber. White wines and Champagne are most vulnerable. Store wine in darkness.

aging.title: Aging & Oxidation
aging.description: Wine ages through slow, controlled oxygen exposure. The bottle, cork, and storage conditions determine whether a wine develops complexity or falls apart.

oxygen_exchange.title: How Wine Breathes
oxygen_exchange.description: Cork allows roughly 1mg of oxygen per year to enter the bottle. This micro-oxygenation drives the slow chemical reactions that transform young wine into something more complex.

process.what_happens: Primary fruit flavors dominate. Tannins are grippy and separate from the fruit. Oxygen begins softening harsh edges.
Tannins polymerize and soften. Primary fruit evolves into secondary flavors. Oxygen integrates components.
Tertiary aromas emerge: earth, leather, mushroom, dried fruit. Color fades. Structure becomes silky.
Fruit fades entirely. Oxidation overtakes the wine. Structure collapses.

bottle_size_impact.title: Size & Aging Speed
bottle_size_impact.description: All corks allow similar oxygen ingress regardless of bottle size. Larger bottles have more wine per unit of oxygen, slowing development.

comparison.note: The reference point for aging curves
Half the aging speed of standard

closure_types.title: Closure & Oxygen
closure_types.description: Different closures allow different amounts of oxygen transmission.

closures.risk: TCA contamination (cork taint) in 2-5% of bottles
Lower TCA risk than natural cork
Possible reduction in very long aging
Inconsistent oxygen transmission; can dry out

oxidation_vs_reduction.title: Oxidation vs Reduction
oxidation_vs_reduction.description: Wine can suffer from too much oxygen (oxidation) or too little (reduction). Both are faults when extreme.

oxidation.title: Oxidation
oxidation.cause: Excessive oxygen exposure from poor storage, dried cork, or too long open
oxidation.prevention: Store on side, maintain humidity, proper cellar temperature

reduction.title: Reduction
reduction.cause: Too little oxygen, often from screwcaps or wines made with minimal sulfur handling
reduction.fix: Often blows off with decanting and air exposure

storage_factors.title: Storage Conditions
storage_factors.description: Proper storage is essential for aging. Poor conditions accelerate decline regardless of bottle quality.

conditions.impact: Every 10F above ideal doubles aging speed. Heat damage is irreversible.
Low humidity dries corks, allowing oxygen ingress. High humidity damages labels but not wine.
UV causes lightstrike. Even fluorescent lights can damage wine over time.
Vibration disturbs sediment and may accelerate chemical reactions.
Keeps cork moist. Screwcaps can store upright.

premature_oxidation.title: Premature Oxidation (Premox)
premature_oxidation.description: A controversial issue, particularly in white Burgundy from the late 1990s-2000s. Wines oxidize years before expected.
premature_oxidation.affected_wines: White Burgundy most famously, but seen in other regions
signs: Deep gold color in young wine
premature_oxidation.note: Premox remains debated. Some believe cork suppliers, others blame winemaking choices. Many top producers have since increased sulfur levels and improved cork selection.

capsules.title: Capsules & Closures
capsules.description: The capsule (foil or cap covering the cork) serves both protective and decorative purposes. Once functional, it's now largely about presentation and tamper evidence.

timeline.purpose: Completely seal cork against air and insects
Protect cork, prevent tampering, exclude insects
Health concerns led to elimination of lead from wine packaging

materials.characteristics: Premium appearance, soft and malleable, recyclable

cutting_debate.title: Where to Cut the Capsule

positions.reasoning: Prevents wine from contacting potentially contaminated capsule material when pouring

positions.position: At the top of the lip

positions.reasoning: Why bother with a partial cut? Just remove it all

cutting_debate.best_practice: Cut below the lip (second ring from top) using a foil cutter, then wipe the rim before and after removing cork

sustainability.title: Environmental Considerations
sustainability.description: Wine bottles represent a significant environmental impact. The industry is slowly adopting lighter bottles, alternative packaging, and recycling initiatives.

carbon_footprint.title: The Weight Problem
carbon_footprint.context: Glass production is energy-intensive, and heavy bottles increase transport emissions. A standard 750ml bottle weighs 400-500g empty; premium bottles can exceed 900g.

comparison.notes: Traditional weight, adequate for most wines

lightweight_movement.title: The Lightweight Bottle Movement
lightweight_movement.description: Some regions and retailers are pushing for lighter glass to reduce carbon emissions from transport.

pioneers.action: Prioritizes light bottles in tender evaluations

pioneers.impact: Proved premium wine doesn't need heavy glass

pioneers.action: Committed to reducing bottle weight across portfolio

lightweight_movement.resistance: Many premium producers resist, arguing consumers equate weight with quality. Studies show this association is learned, not innate.

recycling.title: Recycling Reality

facts.detail: Can be melted and reformed indefinitely without quality loss
Clear, green, and amber glass must be separated for recycling
Each 10% recycled glass added reduces energy by 2-3%
Washing and refilling has lower footprint than recycling, but hygiene and logistics are challenging

what_you_can_do: Remove capsules (they can jam recycling equipment)
Remove corks (natural corks can be recycled separately)
Keep glass separate from other recyclables where required

trends.example: Frugalpac Frugal Bottle (recycled paper outer with food-grade liner)
German Mehrweg system, some natural wine bars
